You will design a data model, expose it with the services, implement some custom logic and deploy to the Cloud Foundry environment, including SAP HANA artifacts.
 
 
You will then create another background service using SAP BTP ABAP environment. This will provide the data for the available incident processors. The data includes some processor information and geo-location. The main data of a processor is assumed to come from an SAP S/4HANA Cloud system (Business Partner data). For simplicity, this data is uploaded only once. In real production scenarios, there should be a more comprehensive integration. To reproduce this scenario, you don't need an access to a real SAP S/4HANA Cloud system as you will use the SAP Business Accelerator Hub Sandbox. In addition, you will create a service using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming model to search for the processors within a given distance range using the power of the SAP HANA database geo-engine.
 
 
To access the data from your front-end applications, you will create SAP BTP destinations.
 
When all background services are available you will use SAP Build Apps to create 2 cross-platform applications. You will use all the power of SAP Build Apps hidden behind the drag-and-drop simplicity, including advanced components, complex (but still intuitive) formulas, REST-based integration for non-standard data requests, and so on.
 

How can I start?

 
Try out the mission in SAP Discovery Center for free. The content of the mission is tested on a regular basis and will be kept up to date by integrating the latest services and features.
 
Even though this mission involves numerous services and applications, you can complete all the steps from start to finish using the SAP BTP Trial Account.
 
You are interested in more end-to-end missions? Pick up one of them from SAP Discovery Center.
 
We are excited to hear your feedback about this mission. Please feel free share your thoughts in the comment section below or reach out to us via Discovery Center support tool.

entry.title: SAP Fiori development newsletter January 2025 (issue #32)
entry.content:
Happy New Year!

Based on what we are seeing in the early days of the year, it promises to be a busy one. SAP is continuing to roll out more AI tools to make it easier to develop applications. To keep you informed, we have our first SAP Fiori Innovation Day of 2025 in Palo Alto on February 5.
https://events.sap.com/sap-fiori-innovation-day-2025/en/home
If you cannot attend, we will have more events in other locations during the year.

For ongoing updates, I encourage you to join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable. You can meet online with fellow developers and the SAP engineers who build our professional development tools. Register online or email me for an invitation. Our next meeting is Wednesday, February 12, 2025. 

If you prefer to receive this in your inbox, please subscribe to the SAP Fiori development newsletter.

 

 

Development News

SAP Fiori tools 2411 update: enhanced full-stack development, AI integration, and more open source

The latest release of SAP Fiori tools introduces several new features that bring together frontend and backend development, expand AI-driven capabilities, and further our journey towards open-source integration. Here’s a look at what’s new and what you can expect.

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-fiori-tools-2411-update-enhanced-full-stack-development-ai-integration/ba-p/13929515

SAP Fiori tools 2501 update: elevating development with AI, usability, and flexibility

The latest enhancements in SAP Fiori tools focus on leveraging AI, simplifying the user interface for adaptations, and improving the overall usability of the tools. This post explains what they mean for your SAP Fiori development experience.

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-fiori-tools-2501-update-elevating-development-with-ai-usability-and/ba-p/13968245

SAP Fiori elements extension: upload Excel files to create new entries using building blocks

This post guides you through the process of implementing an SAP Fiori elements application by leveraging the newly released SAP Fiori elements field building block, which simplifies the implementation compared to the previous method.

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-fiori-elements-extension-upload-excel-files-to-create-new-entries-using/ba-p/13943173

Joule to migrate SAPUI5 apps to TypeScript

As you probably know, Joule helps writing freestyle SAPUI5 code in SAP Build Code. Joule's latest feature offers an AI-driven migration of UI5 applications to TypeScript, which, compared to JavaScript, enables an improved development efficiency. See the details in https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sapui5-meets-typescript-a-smooth-migration-journey-with-joule/ba-p/13958872

Building SAPUI5 Apps for SAP Build Work Zone

This new guide gives a lot of technical insights into SAPUI5 apps running on SAP BTP: You will learn how to structure complex SAPUI5 applications to avoid common pitfalls, how multiple apps work with a shared reuse library and how to integrate business solutions as content providers into SAP Build Work Zone. 
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/designing-ui5-apps-as-business-solution-for-sap-build-work-zone-part-1/ba-p/13923459​

SAPUI5 flexibility to adapt standard SAP apps

SAPUI5 flexibility provides features to align with a clean core approach with on-stack extensions. SAP standard apps remain untouched, no modifications are needed, and even predefined extension points are unnecessary. 
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/extending-sapui5-apps-adaptation-projects-vs-extension-projects/ba-p/13961264

AI is NOT invited to our meetings

In our monthly SAP development roundtable several people have been recording our calls and generating AI summaries. We have received some complaints. Going forward, we ask that you don’t do this. Our goal is for everyone to share openly. Having a recording and/or transcript might prevent this. If we demo something new and exciting, we’ll create a recording to share. Otherwise, please do not invite your AI or chatbot to join. People only!

SAP S/4HANA extensibility: A holistic summary

This series of articles is a holistic summary of extensibility with SAP S/4HANA. It captures the challenges and shows the essence of what we do at SAP, together with partners and customers (beyond providing the business solutions of course).

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/why-s-4hana-extensibility-options-are-crucial/ba-p/13921038

SAP Build Code updates

Read about the latest updates to SAP Build Code. There are enhancements for experts, beginners, and everyone in between.

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/product-updates-for-sap-build-code-november-2024-edition/ba-p/13948533

Behind the curtain on our development processes

SAP UX Engineering is moving from traditional scrum/agile to a product-oriented model. This will redefine how we deliver value. Scrum has brought structure and efficiency, but we are now bringing together teams comprised of Product Management, Engineering, and Design. This will all us to focus on what matters most: customer outcomes over feature output or velocity. Read the first post of the 4-part series

https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/why-ux-engineering-changing-sebastian-steinhauer-pkofe/

Events

SAP Fiori Innovation Day Silicon Valley on Feb 5

 

New innovations make the user experience for SAP S/4HANA intuitive, modern, and highly configurable. People have the information they need at their fingertips and can collaborate with colleagues to make better decisions. It’s easier than ever to adapt apps to meet your needs or to create new ones using our powerful and easy-to-use tools.

In this full-day workshop, we will cover:

  • SAP’s UX strategy and how it is evolving to fulfill the promise of the intelligent, collaborative enterprise
  • Extending SAP S/4HANA while keeping a clean core
  • Building apps using the latest tools and technologies – the same ones SAP uses to build apps for SAP S/4HANA, including a hands-on session
  • How to move your organization forward, including how to take advantage of AI for developing applications

Register now:
https://events.sap.com/noam/sap-fiori-innovation-day-silicon-valley/en/home

 

If you can't make it to Palo Alto, where should we have our next SAP Fiori Innovation Day?

We are planning our 2025 SAP Fiori Innovation Day schedule. Please let us know in which location you might be interested in joining us.

We will not have events in every location, but we want to get a sense of where you are willing to travel to learn about SAP Fiori app development (see topics above).

https://events.sap.com/sap-fiori-innovation-day-2025/en/home

 

SAP Fiori Innovation Day Heidelberg recap

 

SAP Fiori Innovation Day in Heidelberg was a full day of learning, networking, and fun. Read about what you missed. Hope you can join us next time.

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/sap-fiori-innovation-day-in-heidelberg-focused-on-using-ai-to-make-apps/ba-p/13949089

 

ASUG Annual Conference call for papers is now open

SAP Sapphire is scheduled for May 19-21, 2025. If you want to speak at the ASUG Annual Conference, their call for papers is open until February 14. Details on their site:

https://www.cvent.com/c/abstracts/71738f29-f5f6-4b3d-9738-ae643a743457

 

Back issues from the past year

SAP Fiori development newsletter November 2024 (issue #31)

SAP Fiori development newsletter September 2024 (issue #30)

SAP Fiori development newsletter July 2024 (issue #29)

SAP FIori development newsletter May 2024 (issue #28)

SAP Fiori development newsletter March 2024 (issue #27)

SAP Fiori development newsletter January 2024 (issue #26)

entry.title: Overview of all GenAI Options in SAP Build Code
entry.content:
Introduction 

SAP Build Code, the pro-code offering utilizing Joule,  brings cutting-edge generative AI (GenAI) capabilities to developers across a wide array of use cases. These range from UI5 and Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) development to providing assistance on Mobile Development Kit (MDK) projects or HANA development.

Since its general availability in March 2024, which initially highlighted AI features primarily for CAP, Joule's GenAI capabilities have expanded significantly. As new features continue to be introduced, this blog post aims to summarize the current features and the variety of use cases they support. Furthermore, as the approach for the use of different functionalities may differ, we will also provide some recommendations that are specific to some features.

Before introducing the list we will dive shortly into what prompting is as well as talk about the difference between general LLM use and Joule for develompent. (Co-Author: @MarcHuber )

**Note: This blog will be updated from time to time to reflect upcoming and new features (See end of post)

What are Prompts? 

"Prompt" is a verb that means to cause, help, or instruct someone or something to do something. In information technology, "prompt" can refer to the "command prompt". 

Prompts are used in GenAI, including SAP Joule, across several topics. A prompt can be a command, input, or a piece of text provided to initiate a response or action. SAP Joule can utilize prompts as instructions to propose code. 

An example of a simple prompt is: "Create an incident management app." 

 

Importance of well-crafted Prompts in generating Code 

Generative AI models – including the ones used by SAP Joule – use prompts as a point of initiation, where they are given initial instructions or context to generate content. Artificial intelligence capabilities are especially useful for tasks that don’t need to consider things like cultural differences. So, app development is a great candidate for being supported using prompts. Effective prompt engineering implies creating well-structured, directive prompts that steer AI responses to ensure they are accurate and relevant to the context. 

The code generated by SAP Joule significantly depends on the quality of input prompts. Therefore, the precision and relevance of the output can be greatly improved by constructing high-quality prompts that draw out the intended response. Such carefully engineered prompts can notably boost user experience and task coherence. 

It is important to remember that crafting an effective prompt necessitates creativity. It is your responsibility to ensure your expected output is clearly conveyed to the model. This commonly includes comprehension of how the model perceives different prompts and the appropriate rephrasing of them.

 

Differences between general LLM use and using Joule for coding tasks inside  SAP Business Application Studio 

Prompt engineering is essential when using a general LLM to ensure good response quality. Prompt engineering methods such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting have been shown to achieve better results. Chain-of-Thought prompting involves guiding individuals to articulate their reasoning step-by-step, helping them clarify their thoughts and explore the reasoning behind their conclusions. This technique encourages deeper cognitive engagement and can enhance problem-solving and decision-making skills by making the thought process explicit. An example of this could be adding the following to the prompt:

“[prompt question]. Please break down your thought process step-by-step.” 

Joule in SAP Build Code, however, is specialized in supporting pro-code developers in coding tasks. It provides predefined slash commands for a variety of use cases, such as UI5, Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), Mobile, and HANA development. Due to the specialization of Joule, the prompt will already be matched and improved, thus reducing the manual effort to make use of extensive prompt engineering methods. 

This chapter will further depict best practices for using Joule in SAP Build Code. 

 

Be clear, specific, and directive  

Generally, in prompt engineering it is always good to provide more context while being clear on what you want. The more precise information the prompt contains, the better Joule will understand what you really want to achieve. Example:  

  • Vague Prompt: "I need a purchase order tracking application."  
  • Improved Prompt: "Develop a purchase order tracking application where entity 'Purchase Order' is associated to entity 'Supplier'. The 'Purchase Order' consists of the following fields: ID, Quantity, Price. Entity 'Supplier' consist of the fields  ID, Name, Contact Person, Mail Address"  

It should be noted that, instead of adding more and more complexity and context into one prompt where Joule for example is used to generate multiple files (e.g. for UI5 development), in some cases breaking complex tasks into simpler ones may be a better approach.  

 

Provide Examples 

Additionally, examples provide meaningful information on how the output should be structured or content should be generated. It can be extremely helpful in some scenarios, for example in sample data generation: 

  • Before: "Create meaningful sample data for the 'Purchase Order' and 'Supplier' entities.” 
  • Improved Prompt: "Create a set of meaningful sample data for the 'Purchase Order' and 'Supplier' entities. The purchase order data should be targeted at German customers and must use the euro currency symbol (€) and the German abbreviation for quantity units (e.g., 'STK' for Stück). Each Purchase Order ID should be exactly 6 digits long. Include at least 20 records. Example format: Purchase Order - ID: 000109; Quantity: 10 STK; Price: 45,25€."

 

Use of context-awareness 

Joule in SAP Build Code in general is context aware of the project you created in the SAP Build Lobby. Depending on the project type (Full-Stack, Fiori, Mobile), certain slash commands will be suggested. Besides that, Joule can make use of files in the project. Depending on the slash command and the prompt description, only required files are considered in the prompt, thereby eliminating the need to use more input data (code) than necessary for the response generation.  

Furthermore, you can make use of the #-sign in a prompt to include files which should be taken into account by Joule. This feature use will be explained in more detail in the following sections.

 

GenAI Options in SAP Build Code 

Joule offers specific skills for SAP app development with SAP Build Code which can be requested using slash commands (“/”). As each slash command serves a different purpose, the required input as well as the output format from within Joule can vary. Therefore, there are best practices for some of the tasks, which will be highlighted in this section. 

 

CAP 

Joule offers skills for the generation and development of CAP. The following slash commands are suggested when creating a Full-Stack Application Project:

/cap-gen-app

For the purpose of creating a CAP application, which involves different parts such as cds model, sample data, and application logic, this command along with a prompt stating these requirements can be triggered. Joule will then support in refining the different parts and generate the whole application at the end.

The prompt can either be precise and context-rich or one can also start with a more vague prompt, since Joule will give guidance. An example for the latter could be:

  • Example: “Create an incident management application with Customers, Indicents, ReplacementItems, and Products. Provide some meaningful sample date.When an incident title containts the word 'urgent' then automatically add logic to set the incident priority to high. ” 

The results looks as follows:

In the sample above Joule splits the app creation process up into multiple tasks and generated some suggestions for each prompt. This serves as an entry point to then further adapt each prompt with the edit button, depending on the requirements. The play button then subsequently gets enabled and triggers the generation of the code, which can be viewed as staging files. This has to be done for all steps. If satisfactory, the application code can be accepted and all the artefacts will be created in the project. All done within the intial slash command. It should be noted, that this advanced application generation process is only triggered when providing  additional information besides the model data, such as sample data or application logic. For further information on this new feature, check out this blog post. 

 

/cap-gen-model 

Besides the multi-agent support from the abovementioned /cap-gen-app command, /cap-gen-model can be utilizied for the single purpose of creating a data model. Joule will create a CAP model along with some sample data based on the prompt. It is recommended to be clear and precise while providing a good amount of context. The following example highlights how being more specific and providing more context can result in better results. 

  • Example: “ I would like to create an incident management application. Can you do that for me?” 

This may provide already some good looking results. However, as in most cases requirements already exist for the entities, its associations, and fields, being more specific can provide the desired outcomes.

With a more specific prompt: 

  • Example: “Create an app where customers can report incidents. Include a 'Customer' entity with fields Customer ID, Name, Email, and Phone Number. Each 'Incident' should include Incident ID, Description, DateReported (Date), and Status. Each incident also includes replacement items, and each replacement item is linked to a product. The 'ReplacementItem' entity should have fields Replacement ID, Quantity , and ReplacementDate (Date). The 'Product' entity should have fields Product ID, Product Name, Category, and ExpiryDate (Date). Use associations instead of compositions for the relationships between entities.” 

In this example, the named entities are defined with the properties coming from requirements. Providing comprehensive descriptions of entities and associations ensures that Joule closely adheres to the application's context during creation. Since Joule prioritizes the creation of compositions over associations, it is advisable to explicitly specify the use of associations as illustrated in the example above. 

If the generated data from the data model does not meet expectations, there is the option to either regenerate the entire dataset to obtain a potentially more suitable outcome, or preferably, use the /cap-edit-model command to make adjustments. 

 

/cap-edit-model 

By using this command, you can edit an existing data model: 

As displayed in the screenshot, a context variable must be entered with the #-sign, so that Joule knows which data model to modify.  

But it is more than just editing an existing data model. You can do code reviews, add comments and much more. You can easily ask like “Does this code follow the best pratices of CAP?” 

You can find more examples in this blog .

 

/cap-gen-data and /cap-edit-data 

Sample data from the CAP app generation can be generated to match realistic values. For general sample data, a vaguer prompt can be used: 

  • Example: “Create realistic values for the sample data” 

Of course, with more precise information, the data will be matched better to the desired format: 

  • Example: “Create realistic values for the sample data. For the Product Entity, make sure the `criticality` values are in the range of [1(Negative), 2(Critical), 3(Positive)]. [...]” 

For further editing the created sample data or adapting existing sample data of a specific entity, /cap-edit-data slash command should be used. A context variable referencing the csv file with existing sample data has to be provided. 

To ensure the generated data meets expectations, it is crucial to use clear and precise prompts. For instance, indicating specific ranges, formats, or patterns can help tailor the output to match the desired criteria. Do not include any detail about the application logic in the data model as it will be ignored by Joule. 

 

/cap-app-logic 

A further pivotal command in the CAP suite is the /cap-app-logic command, which enables the creation and modification of application logic. This command requires a context variable and an application logic file, serving as prerequisites. For more intricate application logic, breaking down the task into smaller, manageable modifications is advisable. 

 

/cap-unit-test 

To create unit tests, this command should be executed after the application logic has been implemented. Considering the context variable of the application logic file, the provided sample prompt for generating a unit test may already be adequate. If additional test scenarios or modifications are necessary, the slash command can be invoked again to make the required adjustments. For unit tests, provide the appropriate data for positive and negative cases to make sure the tests pass accordingly. 

 

Fiori/UI5 

This section describes the use of slash commands for Fiori development, which can be UI5 freestyle apps, Fiori Elements apps, or a mixture between these two using the flexible programming model. 

/fiori-gen-spec-app 

In addition to /cap-gen-app, the /fiori-gen-spec-app slash command facilitates the rapid creation of SAP Fiori applications by translating business requirements into a CAP project with a SAP Fiori elements application. This command leverages the GenAI capabilities to interpret text or image-based inputs, defining entities, associations, and UI layouts based on your specifications. It's designed to aid developers by generating list reports, object pages, and form entry object pages, all while supporting draft-enabled applications by default. 

Since this creates a whole application, it is suggested to provide enough and precise context of the business requirements to the prompt to ensure accurate generation and thus avoid regenerating the whole project. This is also important as regenerating the project will create a completely new project without being able to take over specific parts. Here, Joule also supports an image/sketch as an input.

  • Example (Text):

 

“ I want to create an SAP Fiori application that shows my customers with their contracts. Each customer has the following attributes: 
1. Name 
2. Identification Number 
3. Address 
4. Contract Information 
5. A contract is referencing a customer. One customer can have multiple 
  contracts. Attributes of a contract include: 
6. Contract ID: A unique identifier for the contract 
7. Customer: Information about the customer involved 
8. Contract Type: The type of contract (sales, service, employment) 
9. Start Date: The date on which the contract becomes valid 
10. End Date: The date on which the contract expires (if applicable) 
11. Status: The current status of the contract (draft, active, expired, terminated, etc.) 

When starting the CAP application I first want to see a list of customers. 
When selecting a specific row in that list, I want to see to a page with details of that customer. 

It should show the following sections: 
- section "Customer Info" displaying the attributes of the selected customer 
- section "Contracts" showing a list of all contracts for this customer 

When selecting a specific row in the contracts list, I want to see a page with details of that contract. This page should show two sections: 

- section "Contract Info" containing: 
 - contract ID 
 - Customer Name 
 - Contract Type 
 - Status 
- section "Validity" containing 
 - Start Date 
 - End Date “

 

  • Example (Image):  

For more information, you can refer to the documentation of this slash command. 

 

/ui5 

The /ui5 slash command offers a broad support for many of the topics resolving around UI5 development. For example, it can be used to create and modify files in a freestyle project. This can range from adding controls and application logic to helping with configuration of the project. Furthermore, if asked, Joule can help understand UI5 related artifacts. Besides freestyle development, it also provides help understand Fiori elements and other UI5 related applications. Being clear and precise is recommended for using this command when generating code. It will try to automatically figure out which files in the project are needed as context and include these. At this stage, however, it does not know anything about e.g. the metadata of a remote service. 

  • Example: “Create a table for the main view based on the entity set ‘Products’ of the OData service, which is represented in the local metadata file. Add the following three columns and bindings to the table: ProductID, Name, Quantity.” 
  • Example: “Add a column to the table in the main view which displays a button called ‘Order’. If the user presses the button, show a message toast with the message ‘Order clicked’” 
  • Example: “What does the code in the main controller do?” 
  • Example: “How can I configure an OData service to be used in the app?” 

Furthermore, the prompt can also consider the currently selected code. For example, if a function inside a controller file is selected, the user can simply ask Joule to explain that code without the need to specify the file in the prompt. 

You can refer to this blog on how to get started with UI5 freestyle development using Joule.

/ui5-typescript 

The SAPUI5 TypeScript assistant can be used for converting a SAPUI5 application project (or single files in it) to TypeScript. It can do both, the creation of the appropriate project setup that handles transpilation, as well as converting JavaScript files to TypeScript, which includes the required structural changes in the files, like using ECMAScript (ES) module imports and ES classes. 

The converted or added files will be listed and can be clicked to inspect a diff view of the changes before accepting them. The diff may be extensive, as such a conversion typically touches many lines. Still, this offers an opportunity to identify any unintended changes. 

A certain amount of re-work of the result may be needed, e.g. to adjust some of the generated return types of functions or to add needed typecasts. 

The functionality itself tries to convert everything inside a project at once. However, in order ut to avoid being hit by size limits and inaccuracies, it is recommended to proceed in smaller steps. For example, first convert only the project setup, but no JavaScript files. Then migrate single or few files at once, depending on their size. It is also recommended to only use this assistant for SAPUI5 application code. 

This assistant is generally available inside SAPUI5 projects. 

  • Example: “Migrate the setup of the project to TypeScript. Do not migrate JS files yet.” 
  • Example: “Migrate the Main controller to TypeScript” 
  • Example: “Migrate the current file to TypeScript” 

Further information can be found in this blog post .

 

/ui5-create-page 

The SAPUI5 Create Page assistant creates a new UI5 view with a corresponding controller and configures routing for seamless navigation to this new page. 

This command scaffolds a new XML view and JavaScript or TypeScript controller, updates the manifest.json to include the necessary routing configurations, and provides default code for controller functionality. It can also add code to existing controllers which trigger the navigation to the new page. It is ideal for quickly setting up additional screens or views in your UI5 project. 

  • Example: “Create a new page with controller and routing setup. The new page should contain an address form, navigation should be triggered by a new button in the header of the Main view.” 

 

Mobile 

For mobile app development using mobile services, Joule also offers slash commands. The prerequisite for using these slash commands is having a mobile app project. 

/mdk-gen-i18n 

This command generates i18n translation files in multiple languages, each containing key-value pairs of strings. Based on your business needs, you can create additional translation files for various languages from a source file (e.g. i18n.properties). This enables you to reach a wide audience and ensure that your application is accessible to users in diverse regions. It is recommended to provide the required languages in the prompt itself: 

  • Example: “Please generate i18n files for the following languages: German, Dutch, Italian, and French.” 
  • Example: “Please generate i18n file in German language.” 

Once accepted, you must choose whether to overwrite or merge the changes. Merge file option append any new changes to existing files. Overwrite file option completely replaces an existing file with the new changes. 

 

/mdk-gen-page  

This command generates a new page containing MDK UI controls. Also, it may also produce other related application definitions, such as Action, other associated Page, Rule or Style files, with the page. The service definition is considered when creating the page. Nonetheless, it is possible to specify the desired layout appearance: 

  • Example: “Please generate a customer list page using object table section where firstname and lastname are displayed as headline. The other labels should be empty. 
  • Example: “Please generate a page displaying customer information in a contact cell control.” 

 

/mdk-gen-action 

This command generates a new MDK action. Also, it may also produce other related application definitions, such as Page or other associated Action files, with the Action. The service definition is considered when creating the Action. 

  • Example: “Please generate an action displaying product information.” 
  • Example: “Please generate an action updating product entity.” 
  • Example: “Please generate an action to call SAP AI chat completions API.” 

Note: All the MDK related slash commands are also accessible for a mobile project created in a full-stack project. 

 

HANA

/hana-gen 

This command enables users to create SAP HANA Cloud projects by specifying requirements in natural language. The tool interprets these requirements and generates a complete project structure, including database artifacts such as tables, views, and sample data. This functionality is particularly useful for rapid prototyping and development, ensuring consistency and reducing manual effort. Example prompts: 

  • Example: “Create an inventory management project and use schema name as 'inventory'.” 
  • Example: “Create a flight management project.” 
  • Example: “Create a bookshop management project and use namespace as 'com.sap' and use schema name as 'bookshop'” 

 

/hana-modeling-help

Joule offers also support on modeling questions, such as calculation views and modeling tasks. This feature can either be triggered trough the slash command or via the BAS command palette : "SAP HANA Modeling: Modeling Help (Joule)".

Here are some example prompts:

  • Example: “How to model a calculation view in BAS?” 
  • Example: "How do I add a data source to a calculation view?"
  • Example: “How do I create an hdbtable in BAS? Please give an example.'"

 

SQL generation using natural language

For the SAP HANA Database Explorer extension in SAP Build Code it is possible to create SQL statements from natural language. The prompt has to be provided in the SQL Concsole and then the execution can be triggered via the "Run Prompt" option. During the SQL statement generation the prompt along with additional information about SAP HANA is utilizied to generate syntetically valid SQL statements. However, the output has of course to be checked and validated by the user.

 

Other Slash Commands 

/help 

The /help command displays a list of prompts available in Joule for SAP Build Code. This includes example prompts and descriptions of each prompt. 

/sap-help

Questions regarding solutions,frameworks, and other information from the SAP Help Portal can be asked from within this command. So, this powerful feature provides access to a huge knowledge source.

/code-search 

Natural language can be utilized to search for code and obtain a concise summary of its functionality. The first time a search is performed, it may take some moments for the results to appear because the workspace content needs to be indexed. The results show the files relevant to the search criteria. 

The next time a code search is performed, Joule suggests prompts based on the previous search. Any new file that has been added to the workspace is indexed for code search. 

 

Inline Code-Completion  

Inline code-completion is a feature that provides code snippet suggestions directly in the editor itself (e.g. for JavaScript) based on your typing input the the context. It will be enabled per default. To use inline code-completion effectively, you can accept a full suggestion by pressing Tab or reject it with Esc. For more control, cmd/ctrl + right arrow (→) can be used to accept suggestions word-by-word. The autocomplete suggestion considers the code around the cursor and recent files to provide a matching suggestion. Furthermore, it may be helpful to write a comment with the desired output to get an autocomplete suggestion: 

  • Example: “// Create a Message Box Popup which has three actions: Save, Draft, and Cancel” 

In the example above, after pressing enter, an autocomplete suggestion will be provided based on the comment.  

 

Final Remarks 

While utilizing GenAI functionality can enhance development efficiency, it is recommended to thoroughly review the proposed code before implementation. Although Joule strives to deliver optimal results, Language Learning Models (LLMs) are not perfect. Additionally, response generation may differ even when using identical prompts. Therefore, if the outcome is unsatisfactory, you may either regenerate the response with the same prompt or revise the prompt to achieve better results.  

For example, if a data schema has been created that mostly meets the requirements but is missing a property or definition, it may be an option to rewrite the prompt by including the provided structure from the last prompt and specifically describing what parts are missing.  

As Joule in SAP Build Code is tailored to specific use cases, a don’t is to ask questions unrelated to the slash commands. An example would be, to ask for the weather, request SAP unspecific text generation, etc. The core strength of Joule within SAP Build Code and the Business Application Studio lies in its powerful tailored capabilities, where a clear and precise prompt will help you with what you want to achieve. 

 

***History - Added features
FeatureAdded
/hana-modeling-help01/2025
SQL generation using natural language01/2025
/cap-gen-model01/2025
/cap-gen-app (Advanced app generation)01/2025
/sap-help01/2025

entry.title: AI in SAP Build - Overview and Best Practices
entry.content:
AI in SAP Build: Transforming Business Applications

Introduction

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing the way businesses operate, and SAP Build is at the forefront of this transformation. SAP Build integrates AI capabilities to enhance application development, process automation, and overall business efficiency. This blog post provides an overview of AI in SAP Build and shares best practices in prompt engineering to help you maximize the potential of AI tools.

AI in SAP Build

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing the way businesses operate, and SAP Build is at the forefront of this transformation. SAP Build integrates AI capabilities to enhance application development, process automation, and overall business efficiency. This blog post provides an overview of AI in SAP Build and shares best practices in prompt engineering to help you maximize the potential of AI tools. 

What is SAP Build? 

SAP Build provides you the flexibility to create and extend apps, automate processes and design digital workspaces with low-code, pro-code and generative AI tools. Its collaborative design allows diverse teams to build solutions faster using prebuilt solutions, AI assistance, and seamless integration with SAP applications. 

General Topic: Best Practices in Prompt Engineering 

Before we start with all the AI features in detail, we have to cover a general topic. Prompt engineering is the art of crafting effective prompts to guide AI models in generating accurate and relevant outputs. For example prompts should be clear, specific, and directive. In this blog you can find all the details about the topic: Prompt Engineering

AI in SAP Build 

Coming to AI in SAP Build. SAP Build is a comprehensive suite designed to simplify the creation and customization of business applications. By incorporating AI, SAP Build empowers users to develop intelligent applications with minimal coding. This is a collection auf all the AI Capabilities in SAP Build. 

SAP Build Code 

  • Overview of all GenAI Options in SAP Build Code

 SAP Build Apps 

  • SAP Build Apps integration with SAP AI Core  

SAP Build Work Zone

  • Joule in SAP Build Work Zone

SAP Build Process Automation (coming soon)

  • Boost Development with GenAI in SAP Build Process Automation  
  • GenAI boosting SAP Build Process Automation  

SAP Build - ABAP Environments (coming soon)

  • ABAP AI  

Joule Studio  (coming soon)

  • TechEd Session about Joule Studio 

 

Conclusion 

AI in SAP Build is transforming the way businesses develop and customize applications, making it easier to integrate intelligent features and automate processes. By following best practices in prompt engineering, you can harness the full potential of AI tools, ensuring they deliver high-quality and relevant outputs. Embrace these strategies to enhance your AI interactions and drive innovation in your business. 

This blog will be constantly updated. 

Share your experience using the AI capabilities in SAP Build in the comments!

entry.title: Developing, Running, and Monitoring LangChain Applications on SAP BTP
entry.content:
This article is translated version of the original Japanese blog:
SAP BTP上でLangChainアプリケーションを開発・実行・監視する

 

Introduction

In today’s rapidly advancing era of generative AI, the demand for applications integrating AI technologies is increasing, aiming to enhance business process efficiency and create added value. SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) has gained significant attention as a flexible and scalable platform for extending SAP S/4HANA Cloud and other business systems. Particularly, building applications leveraging generative AI on SAP BTP is the key to unlocking new possibilities.

This blog provides a step-by-step guide to developing, operating, and monitoring large language model (LLM) applications on SAP BTP. Using the “gpt-4o” model provided by Azure OpenAI as an example, we will cover everything from configuring LLM access using SAP AI Launchpad to deploying LangChain applications with SAP Build Code, and monitoring them using Langfuse. By the end, you will have a clear understanding of how to maximize the potential of SAP BTP to build scalable AI solutions.

We hope this blog helps you learn the technical fundamentals of application development with generative AI and take your first steps toward applying them in real business scenarios.

Steps

Prerequisites

  • SAP AI Launchpad / SAP AI Core (Extended Plan) / SAP Build Code is activated.

  • A Full-Stack Application-type DevSpace is created using SAP Build Code.

1. Accessing Powerful LLMs via Generative AI Hub

Access external powerful LLMs through Generative AI Hub in SAP AI Launchpad. First, check the available models listed in the SAP Note below as needed:

https://me.sap.com/notes/3437766

For this guide, we’ll use the “gpt-4o” model provided by Azure OpenAI.

  1. In SAP AI Launchpad, navigate to the working resource group, and go to “ML Operations” => “Settings” from the left pane. Click “Create” in the top right corner to create an LLM access configuration.

  2. Input the information as shown in the image. The name can be arbitrary; in this case, we’ll use “gpt-4o.”

  3. On the next page, configure the type and version of the LLM to use. Refer to the aforementioned SAP Note for the modelName and modelVersion.

  4. After completing the settings, ensure that the modelName is displayed as “gpt-4o.” Click the “Create Deployment” button in the top right corner based on this startup configuration.

  5. You can configure the startup time and other settings, but proceed with the default settings for this example. Once successful, you should see the following screen. Wait for the LLM access point to be created and activated; its current status should display “Running.”

The setup of Generative AI Hub is now complete.

2. Preparing a LangChain Application with SAP Build Code and Connecting it to Langfuse Pre-deployed on SAP BTP

Next, deploy a LangChain application to SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Runtime using SAP Build Code. In our previous blog, we deployed Langfuse, a monitoring platform for LLM applications, on SAP BTP, Kyma Runtime. If you haven’t yet referred to it, please check it out here:

Deploying Langfuse on SAP BTP, Kyma Runtime

For this guide, clone a sample application from GitHub:

 

 

 

git clone https://github.com/watwatwhat/Langfuse_LangChain_onSAPBTP.git

 

 

 

The application is structured as a Multi-Target Application (MTA), and you can deploy it easily using the default command-line tools available in SAP Build Code.

Key Points in the Source Code:

  • Using Generative AI Hub SDK to utilize LangChain via SAP AI Core.

 

 

 

from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import OpenAIEmbeddings as g_OpenAIEmbeddings
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI as g_ChatOpenAI

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub')  # <- GenAIHubプロキシ
embeddings = g_OpenAIEmbeddings(proxy_model_name=embedding_model)  # <- SAP AI Core経由のOpenAI Embeddingモデル
llm = g_ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name=model_option, proxy_client=proxy_client, temperature=0.0)  # <- SAP AI Core経由のOpenAI Chatモデル

 

 

 

 

  • Sending logs to Langfuse.

 

 

 

agent = create_react_agent(
    llm=llm,
    tools=tools,
    prompt=prompt
)
agent_executor = AgentExecutor(
    agent=agent,
    tools=tools, 
    handle_parsing_errors=True, 
    verbose=True,
    max_iterations=5
)
from langfuse.callback import CallbackHandler
langfuse_handler = CallbackHandler(
    public_key="XXXXXXX",
    secret_key="XXXXXXX",
    host="http://XXXXXXXX:3000"
)

response = agent_executor.invoke({"input": input}, config={"callbacks": [langfuse_handler]})

 

 

 

Retrieve the connection information from Langfuse’s UI by clicking the “Configure Tracing” button on the dashboard page. For use with LangChain, open the LangChain tab and copy the code snippet.

Deployment Steps:

  1. Navigate to the cloned directory and build the project:

 

 

 

mbt build

 

 

 

  1. Deploy the generated mtar file to SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Runtime. After logging in with cf login, specify the target organization and space:

 

 

 

cf login
cf deploy mta_archives/langfuse-test-app_1.0.0.mtar

 

 

 

  1. Once deployment is complete, access the application via the internet. Retrieve the application URL using the following command:

 

 

 

cf apps

 

 

 

For this example, the application is published as “langfuse-langchain-srv,” and the URL will be displayed alongside it. Let’s run the LangChain application.

3. Running Inference Chains with LangChain and Checking Traces on Langfuse

Using Postman installed on your local PC, send requests to this Python application. When executed, the AI agent will perform inference on SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Runtime, and return results.

Translation: 

JapaneseEnglish
3の3乗根は？What is the cube root of 3?
3の3乗根は約1.442です。計算機を使用して求めました。The cube root of 3 is approximately 1.442. It was calculated using a calculator.

Since this example includes a trace handler using Langfuse, check the traces via Langfuse’s UI by navigating to “Tracing” => “Traces.” You will see a list of traces as shown below:

Inspect the details. The right-hand pane displays the sequence of actions performed by the AI agent implemented in LangChain. Selecting the topmost element reveals the input and output in the left-hand pane.

Following the right-hand pane, you can confirm that the “calculator” tool was used. This tool, passed to the AI agent in the LangChain source code, was appropriately selected for the task “What is the cube root of 3?” The AI agent deemed this action suitable and used it.

The statistics are also reflected on the dashboard:

While this scenario only used the calculator tool, real-world use cases can become more complex. The products introduced in this blog will prove even more effective in such scenarios.

Conclusion

This blog introduced the steps to develop, run, and monitor LLM applications using SAP BTP. From configuring Generative AI Hub via SAP AI Launchpad to deploying LangChain applications with SAP Build Code and monitoring them with Langfuse, we hope this guide demonstrated the potential of SAP BTP.

Applications integrated with generative AI bring new value to traditional business processes, enabling more efficient and flexible system operations. Additionally, utilizing monitoring tools like Langfuse allows for detailed analysis of AI behavior and inference results, improving reliability and optimizing operations.

As generative AI and LLM technologies continue to evolve, their applications across various use cases will grow. Through application development based on SAP BTP, we hope you accelerate your organization’s digital transformation. May this blog serve as the first step toward achieving that goal.

We look forward to seeing your continued efforts in leveraging SAP BTP and generative AI-related technologies for new challenges.

entry.title: SAP Build Code Joule Evolves: Unveiling Multi-Agent Support for Advanced CAP Application Generation
entry.content:
As of early 2025, the optimal approach for professional developers working with Generative AI involves creating project skeletons that cover general requirements, followed by incremental enhancements. SAP Build Code Joule expands on this practice with multi-agent support, allowing developers to use a single prompt to engage specialized agents for various tasks. By coordinating these agents, Joule creates more comprehensive project foundations, thereby streamlining the development process. This approach enables developers to focus more on refining and customizing their applications, improving productivity and efficiency.

To align with these best practices for Build Code CAP developers, we've begun supporting more complex requirements for initial CAP application generation.

Note: If you're more used to the previous data model generation process, you can use a newly introduced command specifically for this purpose: /cap-gen-model.

Let's walk through the end-to-end flow to see these improvements in detail.

 

1. Create Project

Create a new Build Code Full-Stack Application from SAP Build Lobby via this navigation path: Create -> Build an Application -> SAP Build Code -> Full-Stack Application.

Note: Choose the 'Node' item from the 'Development Stack' dropdown box to see the new improvements.

Once the status of the newly created application changes from 'Pending Creation' to a normal status, click the application name to load it into the Build Code development environment. By default, the Storyboard will open automatically, and you can access the Build Code Joule panel from the left sidebar.

 

2. Initialize CAP Application

Select the /cap-gen-app command. You'll notice a new example prompt added for the capex application. You can view the full content in the Joule input field once it's selected.

Note:

For backward compatibility, SAP Build Code Joule maintains the previous functionality alongside the new multi-agent approach. You can still use the simpler generation process by typing the /cap-gen-app command without a detailed prompt, which triggers the original single-agent generation. This short-term solution allows you to choose between a comprehensive multi-agent approach for complex applications and a quicker, simpler generation for basic needs. Keep in mind that the richness of your input directly influences the comprehensiveness of the generated output.

Now, instead of showing the generated result for a simple request (only data model-related), an execution plan is displayed. The complex requirements are broken down into subtasks and we apply our best practices. As an end-user, you can review the improved user requirements and enhance them manually (via the pencil action button). This follows a popular human-in-the-loop pattern for working with Generative AI. We believe it's always easier for users to improve requirements using human language rather than adjusting generated code, which may be less familiar.

Once the improved requirements in steps are satisfactory, you can trigger the generation step-by-step manually.

Let's start with step 1 to generate the CAP data model.

After executing step 1, step 2 becomes available. You can check the generated content in the bottom 'Staging Files' UI block. Click any item to open the file content in Build Code.

Note:

  1. The staging files are in a temporary folder for review purposes only. You can manually change them if you're certain there won't be unexpected consequences for the following steps.
  2. You can rerun a step if you encounter any failures or are unsatisfied with the existing content.

Before running step 2 for sample data, let's improve it further to make the sample data more specific. Edit it, save it, and run it.

Then, proceed to step 3 for custom application logic.

You'll see two new files generated per the requirement for this step:

  1. The 'service.js' file: mapping configuration for the application logic handler and service entity events.
  2. The 'before-asserts-create.js' file: new logic attached to the entity 'Asserts' create event, triggered before every assert entity creation.

Background info on how Build Code Joule determines the application logic configuration:

  1. Validation checking logic: typically attached to the entity 'CREATE' or 'UPDATE' event in the 'before' phase.
  2. Entity property value calculation logic: typically attached to the entity 'READ' event in the 'after' phase.
  3. Custom service logic for a CAP action or function: no standard entity event, typically attached to the 'on' phase.

Note:

  1. Describe the app logic requirement more specifically to align with the above guidelines.
  2. You can still change the configuration later (after accepting the staging files) using the CAP application logic editor and adjust the logic content using the Build Code Joule command /cap-app-logic.

When all steps are executed successfully, the 'Accept' button becomes visible under the staging files. Accept the content into your working project if you're satisfied with the staging content.

The Storyboard will refresh to reflect all updated aspects: data model, sample data, service entities, and the application logic attached to the service entity.

You can switch to the detail view of the Storyboard for a better overview of each entity.

 

3. Preview the Application

After initializing the application according to the business requirements, it's time to preview it. Click the green triangle button in the top-right corner to launch it.

The CAP application will launch in local mode with sample data in a new window.

Use the 'View as table' button to check the runtime content for each entity, e.g., the 'Asserts' entity.

Click the 'Create' button to verify the application logic and see if there's a warning when the cost is exceeded. Test with sample input values.

You should see a warning message for a negative use case test and successful creation for a positive use case test.

If the application logic runs as expected, great! However, you may sometimes need to troubleshoot unexpected code issues (including data model, sample data, and FE UI annotations), debug, and ask Build Code Joule to enhance the code or make manual changes.

A Useful Tip: To improve logic codes using Build Code Joule with additional runtime data structure as the reference.

Additional info: In this flow, the cost 'Currency' field is not covered. If you click the value help button beside the cost value, you may find it empty.

Try solving this via manual changes or using Data Editor & Build Code Joule to enhance it. Here's a sample content for reference (/db/data/sap.common-Currencies.csv)

 

name;descr;code;symbol;minorUnit
US Dollar;United States Dollar;USD;$;2
Euro;Euro Member Countries;EUR;€;2
Japanese Yen;Japan Yen;JPY;¥;0
British Pound;United Kingdom Pound;GBP;£;2
Australian Dollar;Australia Dollar;AUD;$;2

 

Once everything looks good, return to Build Code and stop the preview.

As mentioned in the beginning, working with Generative AI on top of initially generated artifacts from Build Code Joule provides a good starting point for further improvements to meet additional requirements. Use Build Code Joule via available commands or visual productivity tools for further changes.

 

4. Deploy the Application

Finally, when the project is ready for deployment, navigate to the Task Explorer to deploy this app to BTP.

You'll be able to access this new CAP service app using a new BTP deployed URL.

Note:

  1. There's no 'View as table' action button for the deployed application, as it's only for local development purposes.
  2. You need create FE (or MDK) UI applications for the service entities separately.

 

P.S.

The SAP Build Code Test Drive tutorial has been updated to showcase this latest feature.

 

Summary

We hope you now have a better understanding of our latest improvements to CAP application initial generation and how it can streamline your development work. In the future, we plan to extend similar support to additional generation scenarios.

Enjoy coding with Build Code Joule assistant!

entry.title: Product Updates for SAP Build Code – January 2025 Edition
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

Welcome to the first SAP Build Code release communications blog post of the new year! We're excited to kick off 2025 with many great new features and enhancements designed to streamline your development experience.

Dive into our new Project Overview providing a streamlined and comprehensive view on your projects. We have also made several improvements to simplify your work with Remote Function Calls (RFC). Another notable addition is the inline editing capability for CDS entities, enabling you to add or edit properties directly in the CDS Graphical Modeler. In addition to these exciting new features, we have also curated a collection of informative blog posts that delve into various aspects related to SAP Build Code.

To explore these features in depth and stay ahead of the curve, check out the details of what's new!

 

The new Project Overview

In our latest release of SAP Build Code, we've introduced an exciting change to the default left pane view. We've switched from the Project Explorer to the new Project Overview. This new Project Overview layout aligns more closely with the Storyboard aesthetics and not only includes all the current functionalities of the Project Explorer but also offers additional capabilities.

As you can see in the screenshot below, the Project Overview presents the following sections: Data Models, Services, Analytical Models, User Interface, and External Resources.

Sections of the Project Overview

You can perform project level actions from the context menu of the project, create new objects of each type from the section ‘+’ action, and perform additional actions from the context menu on each object.


Performing Actions in the Project Overview

Moreover, we are thrilled to announce that in the future the Project Overview will support not just projects created via the SAP Build Code Lobby or those using the "full-stack with productivity tools" template, but it will also support full-stack applications built on SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) Java or CAP Node.js. Stay tuned for these upcoming enhancements, and we hope you enjoy the improved interface!

 

Improvements when Working with RFC

When you are working with Remote Function Calls (RFCs) in your Full-Stack Application Using Productivity Tools projects you should try out the following enhancement: When adding a Remote Function Call to a Full-Stack Application Using Productivity Tools project, the run configuration for the external resource is now updated automatically. You can use the run configuration to select which destination to use for RFC when previewing the full-stack application.

For Remote Function Calls, both basic authentication and now also principal propagation are supported, allowing you to explore services in the Service Center, preview apps via run configuration, and run deployed apps seamlessly without the hassle of entering user credentials. For further information, see Explore SAP System Functions.


Run Configuration: Functions (RFC)

 

Inline Editing for CDS Entities

We are thrilled to announce the launch of our new inline editing feature in the CDS Graphical Modeler! This enhancement allows users to add or edit properties directly within an entity in the diagram shown in the CDS Graphical Modeler. This means fewer clicks, less navigating and a more intuitive experience. This addition complements the existing property sheet functionality and will make your work smoother and faster.


Adding a new property

 

Deprecation of Java Tools in SAP Web IDE

We'd like to inform you of important updates affecting SAP Web IDE: SAP Web IDE sunset will take place as part of the sunset of the SAP BTP, Neo environment by December 31, 2028. As a successor of SAP Web IDE, SAP introduced the SAP Business Application Studio five years ago, which has been serving as a robust and modernized alternative for development needs.

Furthermore, we'd like address the recent announcement regarding the deprecation of the SAP Java Buildpack 1. This component is set to be deprecated from the SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry environment by June 2025. Consequently, Java tools currently supported in SAP Web IDE will also be phased out by the same date in June 2025.

For a more detailed examination of these changes, how it may affect your development process, and guidance on migrating to the recommended environments, please read Liat Borenstein’s comprehensive article: Deprecation of Java Tools in SAP Web IDE.

 

Further readings

  • Overview of GenAI in SAP Build and SAP Build Code 

Since its initial release in March 2024, SAP Build Code has rapidly evolved to bring cutting-edge generative AI (GenAI) functionalities to developers across diverse use cases. The latest blog post of my colleagues David Bizer and Marc Huber delves into the current features of SAP Build Code and the wide array of use cases they support. They also provide recommendations tailored to specific functionalities to ensure you get the most out of this powerful tool.

If you are interested in an overview on generative AI in SAP Build, you shouldn’t miss this blog post written by my colleague Marc Huber: AI in SAP Build - Overview and Best Practices.

  • Multi-Agent Support for Advanced CAP Application Generation

Streamline your development process by using the new feature for multi-agent support for advanced CAP application generation with SAP Build Code Joule. The key: harness specialized agents for various tasks using just a single prompt. In his latest blog post, Xin Ye explains this new feature in detail by guiding through an end-to-end flow.

  • Migrating SAPUI5 Applications to TypeScript with the Help of Joule 

As you probably know, Joule supports you with writing freestyle SAPUI5 code in SAP Build Code. Joule's latest feature offers an AI-driven migration of UI5 applications to TypeScript, which, compared to JavaScript, enables an improved development efficiency. This blog post, written by Martin Haeuser is dedicated to providing insights into the latest feature that offers AI-driven migration of SAPUI5 applications to TypeScript.

  • Differences between Extension Projects and Adaptation Projects in SAPUI5 

Looking to enhance your SAPUI5 applications? The blog post "Extending SAPUI5 apps: Adaptation projects vs. Extension projects" written by my colleague Oliver Graeff, explores the flexibility of SAPUI5, which allows not just developers but also key users and end users to extend standard apps. Discover how developers harness the power of adaptation projects in SAP Business Application Studio, the development environment of SAP Build Code. While adaptation projects offer advanced, strategic solutions for code-based extensions, there are also the predecessor extension projects. To address frequent confusions, Oliver explains the differences between the two of them.

  • Deprecation of the karma-ui5 Plugin 

SAPUI5 provides many testing options, like unit and integration tests, which are summarized in UI5 Testing. As the Karma test runner is deprecated since April 2023, the UI5 team now also has to deprecate karma-ui5. For further details, please read the following blog post from Oliver Graeff:  Deprecation of karma-ui5 plugin.

  • SAP Fiori tools 2501 Update 

SAP Fiori tools is consistently evolving, bringing innovative features that empower both experienced developers and casual users. The recent updates focus on harnessing AI, simplifying the user interface for adaptations, and enhancing the overall usability of the tools. Read this blog post written by Christoph Gollmick and discover how these new enhancements can elevate your SAP Fiori development journey.

  • SAP HANA SQL Notebooks in SAP Build, SAP Business Application Studio and Visual Studio Code

With SQL Notebooks you can incorporate SQL statements into your projects. It offers effortless access to your underlying SAP HANA database or HDI Container but also includes scripts for setup, adding test data, and delivering educational functions. In this blog post, Volker Saggau provides a comprehensive overview of SAP HANA SQL Notebooks in various environments such as SAP Build, SAP Business Application Studio, and Visual Studio Code.

 

For further information on new features, please check our roadmaps: roadmap for SAP Build Code, SAPUI5 Road Map. And have a look at What's New in SAP Build Code, the What's New Viewer for SAPUI5 and the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model Release Notes. If you want to stay up-to-date with news, learning resources, and product and strategy updates for SAP Build Code: follow our tag.

We look forward to seeing how you use these new capabilities to create innovative solutions and drive your projects forward. Stay tuned for more updates and happy coding! 😊

entry.title: How can SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite support your journey to SAP S/4HANA Cloud?
entry.content:
Yesterday, while presenting SAP’s financial results for FY2024, our CEO Christian Klein explained that we exceeded our cloud goals, accelerating cloud revenue and current cloud backlog growth against a much larger base. He also mentioned that half of our cloud order entry are now including AI. Clearly, our aim is to provide our customers with a suite of cloud-based applications that provides the most valuable data in business and powers unique AI capabilities. In this blog post, I would like to present how SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite – both running on SAP Business Technology Platform – are at the heart of SAP’s strategy and support our customers in their journey from legacy ERPs to SAP S/4HANA Cloud.


We know that for many SAP customers, the transition to SAP S/4HANA can be challenging, because someone in the organization has added a lot of custom code to your ERP 😉. That’s why SAP has created RISE with SAP, a packaged offering that helps you transform on-premise SAP ERP systems, modernize business processes, and become more agile in the cloud. And in RISE with SAP, you will find anything you need to fulfill your needs.

Let me be a bit more specific: every company in the world uses software to run their operations. We all know the most efficient and effective way is to use standard software like our SAP S/4HANA ERP. At the same time, we are fully aware that every organization in the world is unique and gains competitive advantage through their very specific processes. This might be the way they run processes for their customers because they offer unique experiences, or the way they engage with their suppliers because they may be faster in the supply chain processes, or it might be their HR processes which allows them to retain the best talent in the market… whatever it is, that makes your company unique, it should be reflected in your software because no one likes manual processes in 2025. In the context of migrating from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA Cloud, following the Clean Core strategy is a powerful opportunity: by eliminating custom code, technical debt, and standardizing the processes and data that underpin ERP operations, your business will benefit from more best practices while being able to customize what it needs to differentiate itself. And when it comes to standardization to achieve clean core with proper extensions and integrations, SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite on SAP BTP enter the game!

SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite add great value to your Cloud ERP

SAP Build allows you to extend and personalize SAP S/4HANA to meet your needs. It accelerates development and automation of extensions with low-code, pro-code, and generative AI tools. It enables stable, secure, and scalable ERP extensions that adhere to a prescribed extensibility model, with compliance to code quality standards and best practices. Tailored for every SAP customer, any size, any landscape, any industry, SAP Build is the best of suite tools for developers and key users collaborating in AI-powered ERP extension development.

Let me say it simply: SAP Build adds great value to your Cloud ERP. You will get more out of your cloud ERP investment with SAP Build. Just because it lets you easily customize, extend, integrate and automate business processes in your SAP S/4HANA Cloud solution to meet specific requirements while keeping the core clean. Watch our webinar “Extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud with SAP Build: your Free Toolkit”: in an hour, you will discover how you can leverage SAP Build’s low-code, pro-code, and generative AI development tools to drive innovation, automate processes, and supercharge your S/4HANA Cloud experience.

SAP Integration Suite is an iPaaS that helps you quickly integrate on-premises and cloud-based processes, services, applications, events, and data. For sure, it also adds value to your ERP: it connects business processes with proven pre-built integrations, APIs, connectors, and best practices. It provides upgrade-stable interfaces, proper monitoring and error resolution capabilities, and actively used and well-documented connectors.

For What Use Cases?

Let me give you very concrete examples where SAP Build is very helpful to address specific challenges in your Lead to Cash processes:

  • Digitize in-store cash sales processing
  • Automate the creation of sales orders from Excel files
  • Automate mass maintenance of billing blocks
  • Automate the approvals of return requests
  • Automate the creation of sales orders from unstructured data in SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • Automate managing credit blocks on sales orders

and more…

I can also share many use cases that could improve your Source to Pay processes:

  • Centralize and streamline supplier communications with dedicated business sites
  • Mass maintenance of purchase contracts
  • Automate the creation of purchase requisitions from structured data in SAP S/4HANA
  • Automate the processing of paper-based invoices
  • Automatically populate missing material codes in purchase requisitions
  • Track sustainability data across your supply chain

and more…

You will find complementary use cases on our SAP Build web page.

And there is more: on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, you can browse 500+ pre-built content packages for SAP Build and 600+ pre-built content packages for SAP Integration Suite. If you have developed custom code in your on-premises ERP in the past to address such use cases or specific needs, you can explore this vast catalog to see if some of these packages might be a good fit for your cloud ERP migration process. Indeed it is very important to know that SAP Build comes with pre-built content for ECC: you can use these SAP Build packages on ECC already to standardize some extensions, and then all the extensions developed on ECC with SAP Build will taken to S/4HANA during the migration.

Insights from Customers

What I presented above is not only theoretical: customers who have been through this SAP S/4HANA journey share valuable insights that can really help you. I have selected two few interesting stories, in different industries.

Sony Pictures

Let me start with Sony Pictures Networks: relying on the legacy system, SAP ECC, their highly customized and siloed infrastructure struggled to align with the company's integrated business processes. And decentralized data presented SAP challenges in consolidating business reports until the end.

Sony decided to remaster their operations for the future through next-generation ERP, with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, and Enterprise Automation, combining SAP Signavio, SAP Build And SAP Integration Suite. Indeed, SAP Signavio radically accelerates business process transformation, and I engage you to read this blog post explaining how customers on SAP ECC systems can prepare for an SAP S/4HANA transformation with SAP Signavio.

“By migrating to SAP S/4HANA and adopting SAP Business Technology Platform, we can keep the core clean. This strategy allows for faster development of any new technologies or any new process rollouts and enables us to take advantage of generative AI as part of our way forward” explains Mahesh Patil, SVP and Head of IT – Infrastructure, Integration, and Data Lake, Sony Pictures Networks India. And the results are excellent:

  • 100% automated process mining, reducing effort from weeks to days
  • 50% of interfaces managed through SAP Integration Suite
  • ~85% faster mobile app creation, reducing effort from four weeks to three days

Hitachi

Would you like another good example? Let’s take Hitachi High-Tech Corporation: they replaced their highly customized multinational business landscape by investing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud in a two-tier public and private cloud model. With a fit-to-standard ethos, the company can now focus on supporting business innovation across its subsidiaries using a side-by-side approach on SAP Business Technology Platform.

“Our new policy is to fit our business processes to standard as much as possible, reducing our need for customizations” says Yuri Yasuda, Manager Corporate DX Department, Digital Transformation Business Group, Hitachi High-Tech Corporation, who shares impressive KPIs:

  • 94% reduction in customization footprint
  • 18x faster handling of solution updates
  • 100% of on-site maintenance requirements eliminated

You can also find inspiration in the S/4HANA projects that Mahindra Group, PX Energy, LEOCH, Farys, Delta Light or Prestige have successfully implemented.

Start your S/4HANA journey with the support of SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite!

I hope that you’re now convinced that SAP BTP in general and SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite in particular are very powerful to ease your migration to SAP S/4HANA. To start this journey, watch our short video and request a customized demo: together, let’s make your business unstoppable!

entry.title: SAP Developer News, January 30th, 2025
entry.content:
Description

What’s New in SAP Build: Q4 2024 Webinar

  • Webinar Recordings (for Public) https://workzone.one.int.sap/site#workzone-home&/groups/zJfVxkPC5b2kQfs6YahqyX/content?folder_id=TfxCd7kH9hdfZ1hdvxFyBU&view_mode=list
  • Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-builders-blog-posts/what-s-new-in-sap-build-q4-2024-edition/ba-p/13980162

SAP Build Code – What’s New January 2025

  • Product Updates for SAP Build Code – January 2025 Edition: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/product-updates-for-sap-build-code-january-2025-edition/ba-p/13993021
  • Deprecation of Java Tools in SAP Web IDE: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/deprecation-of-java-tools-in-sap-web-ide/ba-p/13997766
  • SAP Build Code Joule Evolves: Unveiling Multi-Agent Support for Advanced CAP Application Generation: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-build-code-joule-evolves-unveiling-multi-agent-support-for-advanced-cap/ba-p/13995395

Upcoming CAP January Release 2025

  • npm @SAP/cds-dk https://www.npmjs.com/package/@sap/cds-dk
  • CAP Release page January 2025 | capire

SAP Community active on Bluesky social media

  • SAP Community Account on Bluesky: https://bsky.app/profile/sapcommunity.bsky.social
  • Starter Pack SAP Community:  https://bsky.app/starter-pack/sapcommunity.bsky.social/3lfnowo7ykb2l
  • Starter Pack SAP Developer Advocates:  https://bsky.app/starter-pack/sapcommunity.bsky.social/3lgbebsq43a2x
  • Starter Pack SAP Champions:  https://bsky.app/starter-pack/sapcommunity.bsky.social/3lgbesf6hqa2r
  • Starter Pack SAP Mentors:  https://bsky.app/starter-pack/sapcommunity.bsky.social/3lgbem6f4u32p

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 What’s New in SAP Build: Q4 2024 Webinar

2:13 SAP Build Code What’s New Jan 2025

5:11 Upcoming CAP January Release 2025

6:14 SAP Community active on Bluesky social media

TRANSCRIPTION

[Intro]This is the SAP Developer News for January 30th, 2025.

[Shrini] Hello SAP developers. I hope many of you got a chance to watch the webinar yesterday on the new SAP Build features released in Q4 2024. There were updates on product-specific highlights on tools such as SAP Build Process Automation, build code, build apps, and WorkZone with some cool demos on the new features. Here are some of my favorites. The design time UI in build apps has been redesigned allowing for a more intuitive user experience. Different pages in an app are easily accessible via drop down. Less frequently used tasks are positioned under app settings. Preview and publish options are now more prominent. In the process automation, now you can create and manage process variants. Process variants allow a variant template developer to create processes with pre-configured settings and a variant management user, usually a business configuration expert can modify the template variant to suit his needs which changes like change or order of variance, modify the start condition, release and deploy variants, etc. In WorkZone standard edition, the integration of Joule is a game changer. Joule in WorkZone provides answers and insights from any connected system via prompts, eliminating the need to switch between different tools and user interfaces. Note that this feature is not available in the free trial. Joule integrated in SAP Build Code, now it's seamless migration of SAP UI5 applications from JavaScript to TypeScript. SAP Build Code has an adaptation editor tool which is a new tool for extending SAP UI5 applications. It replaces the SAP UI5 Visual Editor. There are many more features. release in the last quarter, do check out the webinar recording and the blog post explaining all the features, the link to which are in the description below.

[Thomas] My colleague, Shri has already brought you some of the news about SAP build enhancements that were released in January of this year. I want to do a little breakout there on specific enhancements for SAP build code. Of course, build code is part of SAP build build and a little bit. bit of the news was covered in his segment, but for professional developers, I want to give you a little additional information. First of all, in SAP Build Code, we see the new project overview. This is to really replace the project Explorer and give you a better visualization of your project and in particular match up the structure to what you're seeing when you're using the storyboard editor. Next, we see improvements when working with remote function calls. There we've added the ability for the run configuration to be updated automatically as well as support both basic and principle propagation. Next, we see the inline editing for CDS entities. This allows you to edit entities and their attributes without having to switch between the graphical editor and the text or code-based editor. And we see the deprecation of Java tools for SAP WebIDE Now, this is an important note because this has to do with the deprecation of the SAP Java BuildPack version 1. Now, that deprecation is going to take place in June of 2025, and at that time, the older tools in the SAP Web IDE for Java development will also be deprecated. Now, this just means that you need to be using the SAP Business Application Studio, which we recommend that you use anyway. So by June 2025, please plan on moving your CAP Java and other Java-based, uh, tooling-based projects to the business application studio. And finally, the last item has to do with the Joule code generative, uh, capabilities in SAP build code.
